reasoning: The 2025-03-01 value for the Cleveland Fed 10-year real interest rate is 1.859%. This is below the recent 12-month average (roughly 1.95%), and lower than 9 of the last 13 monthly observations. In series that exhibit mean reversion or stationary noise, a dip below the median typically pushes the near-term probability of an increase above 50%. The FRED tool’s statistical analysis yields a combined probability of 0.57 for the first month out, reflecting both simple linear extrapolation and seasonal/historical patterns. Over a longer horizon, macro expectations suggest further Fed rate cuts in late 2025 and early 2026, which are likely to push nominal (and real) interest rates lower across the yield curve. Because of this anticipated downward drift, the initial likelihood of an increase (driven by mean reversion) will gradually fade over time. As mandated, my uncertainty increases with the forecast horizon, pulling the probability estimates progressively closer to 50% for the distant resolution dates. I estimate 57% for April, stepping down sequentially to 50% by March 2026.
